did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach called SkinXNet, which is a 37-layer 2D convolutional neural network (CNN) based on a ResNet structure, combined with Difference of Gaussian (DoG) feature enhancement. This model was used for the automatic classification of skin lesions (specifically Melanoma vs. Melanocytic nevus) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning model called SkinXNet for automatic detection of skin lesions. It details a specific methodology (DoG feature enhancement combined with ResNet), describes the dataset used (ISIC 2019), and reports original experimental results including sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and AUC metrics. Although published in a conference proceedings (IPRIA 2021), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: SkinXNet: A DoG-based Model for Automatic Detection of Skin Lesion using Deep Learning
journal: 2021 5th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Image Analysis (IPRIA)
brief_summary: This study proposes SkinXNet, a 37-layer 2D convolutional neural network based on a ResNet structure, to automatically classify Melanoma from Melanocytic nevus using dermoscopic images. The model incorporates Difference of Gaussian (DoG) feature enhancement to address challenges such as poor contrast and intraclass heterogeneity. The approach was validated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, specifically using a balanced subset of 4001 images for Melanocytic nevus and 4522 for Melanoma. Evaluation metrics included sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and AUC, with confidence intervals calculated using DeLong’s and Wilson’s techniques. The proposed framework achieved a sensitivity of 0.93, an AUC of 0.815, and an accuracy of 81%, demonstrating superior sensitivity compared to previous studies. The results suggest that this hybrid approach can effectively support medical experts in the early detection of melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes studied in the binary classification task. The paper focuses on detecting Melanoma from Melanocytic nevus. It had 4522 images in the original ISIC 2019 subset used, with 4001 selected for the balanced training set. The model achieved a sensitivity of 0.93 for this class.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. It had 12875 images in the original ISIC 2019 subset used, with 4001 selected for the balanced training set to address class imbalance. The model's performance for this class was slightly reduced compared to Melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between Melanoma and Melanocytic nevus
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a binary classification task to distinguish between Melanoma and Melanocytic nevus using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The model outputs a probability determining the category of the skin lesion. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, accuracy, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2019 dataset, a publicly accessible collection of quality-controlled dermoscopic images of skin lesions gathered from leading health centers worldwide. The original dataset contained 25,331 images across nine diagnostic categories. For this study, a subset focusing on Melanoma and Melanocytic nevus was used.

use.training: Used for training the SkinXNet model. The original unbalanced subset (4522 Melanoma, 12875 Melanocytic nevus) was balanced by selecting only 4001 images for Melanocytic nevus, resulting in a balanced training set of 8002 images (4001 per class). Images were resized to 250x250 pixels.
use.other: The model was evaluated on the collected data from the ISIC 2019 challenge. The paper does not explicitly specify a separate held-out test set from this dataset, but reports final performance metrics (Sensitivity 0.93, AUC 0.815, Accuracy 81%) on the data used for evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate 95% confidence intervals for the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
To evaluate the variability and calculate 95% confidence intervals for sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA, p-values) for comparing the proposed model against others. It reported performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC) with 95% confidence intervals calculated using DeLong’s and Wilson’s techniques, but these were used for estimating variability rather than for formal hypothesis testing of differences between models.

ISIC 2019.notes: The model was trained on a balanced subset of 8,002 images (4,001 Melanoma, 4,001 Melanocytic nevus) from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The holdout test set consisted of 8,523 images total (5,454 training, 1,705 test, 1,364 validation). The proposed SkinXNet achieved the highest sensitivity (0.93) and F1-score (0.83) for Melanoma compared to previous studies listed in Table II (e.g., Rashid et al., Nasiri et al., Yu et al., Gessert et al.).

architecture_details.backbone_structure: Slightly stronger version of 2D residual network architecture with residual blocks containing three convolutional layers
block_types: Identity block (modified standard block where input activation dimension matches output)
Convolutional block (similar to identity block but with 2D convolutional layer followed by batch normalization in skip connections)
architecture_details.initial_layers: 7x7 convolutional layer followed by 3x3 max-pooling layer
architecture_details.stage_composition: Second stage: 1 convolutional block + 2 identity blocks; Third and fourth stages: 3 identity blocks each

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale normalization applied to lessen the impact of illumination diversity and enhance training convergence speed.
Difference of Gaussian (DoG) filtering applied for edge detection and feature enhancement to distinguish specific frequency ranges where masses appear.
Image normalization by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: To address class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset, the number of Melanocytic nevus images was reduced from 12,875 to 4,001 to match the 4,522 Melanoma images, resulting in a balanced training set of 8,002 images (4,001 per class).
Applied to lessen the impact of illumination diversity and enhance training convergence speed by mapping pixel values to the [0...1] range.

Used as a feature enhancement technique for edge detection. It involves subtracting two Gaussian-smoothed versions of the image with different standard deviations to distinguish specific frequency ranges where masses appear.
Images were normalized by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation.
All images were resized to 250-by-250 pixels.

conclusions: The proposed SkinXNet framework, which combines Difference of Gaussian (DoG) feature enhancement with a ResNet-based deep residual architecture, effectively addresses challenges such as poor contrast, intraclass heterogeneity, and visual resemblance in skin lesion images.
SkinXNet achieved a sensitivity of 0.93, an AUC of 0.815, and an accuracy of 81% on the ISIC 2019 dataset for binary classification of Melanoma versus Melanocytic nevus.
The proposed model demonstrated superior sensitivity for Melanoma identification compared to previous studies and state-of-the-art deep learning methods evaluated in the paper.
The use of DoG edge detection for feature enhancement contributed to higher sensitivity and F1-score for Melanoma, although performance for Melanocytic nevus was slightly lower (sensitivity 0.70).
The hybrid approach of DoG feature enhancement and deep learning is considered useful for providing a second opinion to medical experts in the early detection of melanoma.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance by subsampling the Melanocytic nevus class (reducing 12,875 images to 4,001) to match the Melanoma class (4,522 images), which the authors acknowledge weakened performance for the nevus class.

The model was trained on a balanced subset of 8,002 images, but the total dataset size used for the experiment was 8,523 images (5,454 training, 1,705 test, 1,364 validation), which is significantly smaller than the full ISIC 2019 dataset.

Performance on Melanocytic nevus was notably lower (Sensitivity 0.70, F1-score 0.79) compared to Melanoma (Sensitivity 0.93, F1-score 0.83), indicating a bias towards the target class due to the balancing strategy.
The paper does not report external validation; results are derived solely from the ISIC 2019 dataset, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The authors note that resizing images to 250x250 pixels and reducing feature dimensions to manage memory usage may have caused the loss of significant data and prominent features.
No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; statistical significance was inferred through 95% confidence intervals calculated via DeLong’s and Wilson’s techniques.

The study is a conference paper (IPRIA 2021) with limited scope compared to journal publications, and the authors explicitly state future work will involve combining multiple datasets to improve accuracy.

The model relies on a hybrid approach of Difference of Gaussian (DoG) feature enhancement and a ResNet-based architecture, which may introduce dependency on specific preprocessing parameters (standard deviations of Gaussians) not fully optimized or reported in detail.
